subject: Physics
q: From a solid sphere of mass M and radius R, a spherical portion of radius R2 is removed as shown in the figure. Taking gravitational potential V=0 at r=∞, the potential at the centre of the cavity thus formed is (G=gravitational constant)
question: From a solid sphere of mass M and radius R, a spherical portion of radius R2 is removed as shown in the figure. Taking gravitational potential V=0 at r=∞, the potential at the centre of the cavity thus formed is (G=gravitational constant)
solution:
Central idea is, consider the cavity as negative a mass and apply the superposition of gravitational potential.

Consider the cavity formed in a solid sphere as shown in the figure.

The gravitational potential at infinite distance,

V=-GMr

⇒V∞=0

According to the question, we can write potential at an internal point P due to complete solid sphere,

Vs=-GM2R33R2-R22

=-GM2R33R2-R24

=-GM2R311R24=-11GM8R

Mass of removed part

=M43×πR3×43πR23=M8

The potential at a point P due to removed part

VC=-32×GM8R=-3GM8R

Thus, potential due to the remaining part at the point P,

VP=Vs-Vc=-11GM8R--3GM8R

-11+3GM8R=-GMR

subject: Physics
q: A space ship of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is launched into a circular orbit close to the earth surface. The additional velocity to be imparted to the space ship in the orbit to overcome the gravitational pull will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ):
question: A space ship of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is launched into a circular orbit close to the earth surface. The additional velocity to be imparted to the space ship in the orbit to overcome the gravitational pull will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ):
solution:
The orbital velocity of satellite is 

vorb=GMR

The escape velocity is 

vesc=2GMR

Hence, the additional velocity required is 

Δv=vesc-vorb=2GMR-GMR=(2-1)GMR
=(2-1)gR
=(2-1)10×6400×103
=8(2-1) km s-1

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration due to gravity on the earth's surface at the poles is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> and angular velocity of the earth about the axis passing through the pole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. An object is weighed at the equator and at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> above the poles by using a spring balance. If the weights are found to be same, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is: (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth)
question: The acceleration due to gravity on the earth's surface at the poles is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> and angular velocity of the earth about the axis passing through the pole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. An object is weighed at the equator and at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> above the poles by using a spring balance. If the weights are found to be same, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is: (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth)
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h above the pole

is given by

gh=g1-2hR  ...(1)

Acceleration due to gravity at the equator due to rotation of the earth is given by

g'=g−ω2R cos2λ  ...(2)

At the equator, λ=0

⇒g'=g−ω2R  ...(3)

As both weights are equal, i.e.; gh=g'

From equation (1) and (2), we get,
⇒ω2R=2gRh
∴h=R2ω22g

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows an elliptical path ABCD of a planet around the sun S such that the area of triangle CSA is 14 the area of the ellipse. (see figure) with DB as the major axis, and CA as the minor axis. If t1 is the time taken for the planet to go over path ABC and t2 for path taken over CDA then:
question: The figure shows an elliptical path ABCD of a planet around the sun S such that the area of triangle CSA is 14 the area of the ellipse. (see figure) with DB as the major axis, and CA as the minor axis. If t1 is the time taken for the planet to go over path ABC and t2 for path taken over CDA then:
solution:
Using Kepler's law,

Area ABCDA=x.

Area SABCS=x4+x2=3x4.

Area SADCS=x2-x4=x4.

Since Arial velocity remains constant. Therefore, area swept will be proportional to time.


3x4x4=t1t2
⇒t1=3t2

subject: Physics
q: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> move round the earth in the same orbit. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is twice the mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The quantity which is same for the two satellites will be
question: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> move round the earth in the same orbit. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is twice the mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The quantity which is same for the two satellites will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the speed of a satellite while revolving around the Earth is given by

v=GMeR   ...1

where, v is the speed, G is the Gravitational constant, Me is the mass of the Earth, R is the radius of the orbit.

As can be seen from the equation, speed is independent of mass of satellite.

Hence, the speed of the satellites will be the same.

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is the time period of simple pendulum on the earth's surface. Its time period becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>T</mi></math> when taken to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> (equal to earth's radius) above the earth's surface. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is the time period of simple pendulum on the earth's surface. Its time period becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>T</mi></math> when taken to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> (equal to earth's radius) above the earth's surface. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be:
solution:
Time period of a simple pendulum at Earth's surface is given by

T=2πlg.

When distance from centre of Earth, i.e., at h=R, acceleration due to gravity is g'=g1+hR2=g22=g4

Now, time period is  xT=2πlg4

⇒xT=2×2πlg

⇒xT=2T⇒x=2

Thus, x=2.

subject: Physics
q: Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is ' $g$ '. If the diameter of earth is reduced to one third of its original value and mass remains unchanged, then the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth is $\ldots\ldots$ g.
question: Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is ' $g$ '. If the diameter of earth is reduced to one third of its original value and mass remains unchanged, then the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth is $\ldots\ldots$ g.
solution:
$\because$ acceleration due to gravity on surface is given by
$\mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}^2}$

Now since diameter is reduced to $1 / 3^{\text {rd }}$, radius also reduces to $1 / 3^{\text {rd }}$, keeping mass constant
New value of acceleration due to gravity on Earth's surface is
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}{3}\right)^2}=9 \frac{\mathrm{GMe}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}^2}=9 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Physics
q: An astronaut takes a ball of mass $m$ from earth to space. He throws the ball into a circular orbit about earth at an altitude of $318.5 \mathrm{~km}$. From earth's surface to the orbit, the change in total mechanical energy of the ball is $x \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{21 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}$. The value of $x$ is (take $\left.\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}=6370 \mathrm{~km}\right)$ :
question: An astronaut takes a ball of mass $m$ from earth to space. He throws the ball into a circular orbit about earth at an altitude of $318.5 \mathrm{~km}$. From earth's surface to the orbit, the change in total mechanical energy of the ball is $x \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{21 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}$. The value of $x$ is (take $\left.\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}=6370 \mathrm{~km}\right)$ :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{h}=318.5 \approx\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}{20}\right) \\ & \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}} \\ & \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}+\mathrm{h}\right)}=\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}+\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}{20}\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{-10 \mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{21 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}\end{aligned}$
Change in total mechanical energy
$\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{TE}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{TE}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{\operatorname{Re}}\left[1-\frac{10}{21}\right]=\frac{11 \mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{21 \operatorname{Re}}\end{aligned}$

The gravitational pull <math><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>G</mi></mrow></msub></math> on the astronaut is

subject: Physics
q:
When one moves from a point $16$ km below the earth's surface to a point $16$ km above the earth's surface. The change in $g$ is approximately $\alpha$ %. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
(Take radius of the earth $= 6400$ km.)
question:
When one moves from a point $16$ km below the earth's surface to a point $16$ km above the earth's surface. The change in $g$ is approximately $\alpha$ %. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
(Take radius of the earth $= 6400$ km.)
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at a depth $d$ below the earth's surface is given by:

$g_d = g\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{R}\right)$

The acceleration due to gravity at a height $h$ above the earth's surface (for $h \ll R$) is given by:

$g_h = g\left(1 - \dfrac{2h}{R}\right)$

Given $d = 16$ km and $h = 16$ km, we can find the values of $g_d$ and $g_h$:

$g_d = g\left(1 - \dfrac{16}{6400}\right) = g\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{400}\right)$

$g_h = g\left(1 - \dfrac{2 \times 16}{6400}\right) = g\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{400}\right)$

The magnitude of the change in acceleration due to gravity when moving from the depth $d$ to the height $h$ is:

$\Delta g = g_d - g_h = g\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{400}\right) - g\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{400}\right)$

$\Delta g = g\left(\dfrac{2}{400} - \dfrac{1}{400}\right) = \dfrac{g}{400}$

The percentage change in $g$ is approximately:

$\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100 = \dfrac{\dfrac{g}{400}}{g} \times 100 = \dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25\%$

Thus, the value of $\alpha$ is $0.25$.

Answer: $0.25$

subject: Physics
q: Escape velocity of a body from earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the radius of a planet be one-third the radius of earth and mass be one-sixth that of earth, the escape velocity from the plate is:
question: Escape velocity of a body from earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the radius of a planet be one-third the radius of earth and mass be one-sixth that of earth, the escape velocity from the plate is:
solution:
Given: RP=RE3, MP=ME6

Escape velocity is given by, v=2GMR

Therefore,

vE=2GMERE ...(i)

vP=2GMPRP...(ii)

⇒vEvP=2

⇒vP=vE2=11.22=7.9 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: Three identical spheres of mass m , are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length a. When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time $\mathrm{T}=4$ seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length 2 a and also the masses of the spheres are made 2 m , then they will collide after _____ seconds.
question: Three identical spheres of mass m , are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length a. When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time $\mathrm{T}=4$ seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length 2 a and also the masses of the spheres are made 2 m , then they will collide after _____ seconds.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{m}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]^y[\mathrm{~L}]^{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{L}^{3 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{T}^{-2 \mathrm{y}} \\ & \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y} \\ & -2 \mathrm{y}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{x}=-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \mathrm{z}=-3 \mathrm{y}=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$
Hence
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{~m}^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{G}^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{a}^{3 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{~T} \propto\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}^3}{\mathrm{~m}}\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{~T}=4 \times\left(\frac{2^3}{2}\right)^{1 / 2}=8 \mathrm{~s}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
question:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
solution:
The variation of the acceleration due to gravity with height h is given by,

g'=g1+hR2.

Since the weight at given height is 13 of the weight at the surface

⇒g'=g3⇒g1+hR2=g3⇒1+hR=3⇒h=R3-1=6400×1.732-1=4685 km

q: The time period of a satellite revolving around earth in a given orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> hours. If the radius of orbit is increased to three times its previous value, then approximate new time period of the satellite will be
question: The time period of a satellite revolving around earth in a given orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> hours. If the radius of orbit is increased to three times its previous value, then approximate new time period of the satellite will be
solution:
From Kepler's Third Law(The Law of Periods), we know

T2∝R3 ⇒T22T12=R2R13=3RR3

Therefore, T2=27T1

=5.19×7≈36 hours

v0x2=v1x1

v1=v0x2x1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The angular speed of the moon in its orbit about the earth is more than the angular speed of the earth in its orbit about the sun.
Reason (R): The moon takes less time to move around the earth than the time taken by the earth to move around the sun.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The angular speed of the moon in its orbit about the earth is more than the angular speed of the earth in its orbit about the sun.
Reason (R): The moon takes less time to move around the earth than the time taken by the earth to move around the sun.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: (A) is correct but (R) is not correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of A.
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of A.
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
solution:
It is known that

ω=2πT⇒ω∝1 T

As,
Tmoon ≈27 days
Tearth ≈365 days
⇒ωmoon >ωearth 

Hence, Both the assertion and the reason are correct and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.

q: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are revolving in circular orbits around a planet with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>I</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of speed of satellite <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to the speed of satellite <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in their respective orbits would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
question: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are revolving in circular orbits around a planet with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>I</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of speed of satellite <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to the speed of satellite <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in their respective orbits would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
solution:
Orbital velocity of a satellite in circular motion around a planet,

vo=GMR∝1R

Now, v1v2=R2R1=8003200=12

Hence, the value of x=2.

subject: Physics
q: The masses and radii of the earth and moon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. Their centres are at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> apart. Find the minimum escape velocity for a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>  to be projected from the middle of these two masses :
question: The masses and radii of the earth and moon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. Their centres are at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> apart. Find the minimum escape velocity for a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>  to be projected from the middle of these two masses :
solution:
On conserving mechanical energy. The initial total energy is equal to final total energy,  ⇒-GM1r2+GM2r2m+12mve2=0
⇒ve=4GM1+M2r.

subject: Physics
q: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
question: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h is given by, g'=g1+hR2

For distance from the centre r=54R height h=R4,

⇒g'=g1+142=1625g

Change in weight 

∆W=mg-mg'⇒∆W=mg1-1625

⇒∆W=925mg

Percentage change =925×100%

=36%

[ Given : radius of earth =6400 km, mass of earth =6×1024 kg ]
question:
Two satellites A and B of masses 200 kg and 400 kg are revolving round the earth at height of 600 km and 1600 km respectively. If TA and TB are the time periods of A and B respectively then the value of TB-TA :

[ Given : radius of earth =6400 km, mass of earth =6×1024 kg ]
solution:
⇒T=2πr3GM

⇒TA=2π6400+600×103GM

⇒TB=2π×10983GM

⇒TB-TA=2π109GM88-77

=314×4.107

=1289.64

=1.289×103 s

subject: Physics
q: Four identical particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are kept at the four corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the masses by the other masses is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>32</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>, the length of the sides of the square is
question: Four identical particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are kept at the four corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the masses by the other masses is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>32</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>, the length of the sides of the square is
solution:
Resultant force, Fnet=2F+F', where

F=Gm2a2 and F'=Gm22a2

Therefore, Fnet=2Gm2a2+Gm22a2

Comparing with the expression given in the question, we get

22+132Gm2L2=Gm2a222+12

⇒a=4L

subject: Physics
q: If earth has a mass nine times and radius twice to the of a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be the minimum velocity required by a rocket to pull out of gravitational force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, where ve is escape velocity on earth. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: If earth has a mass nine times and radius twice to the of a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be the minimum velocity required by a rocket to pull out of gravitational force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, where ve is escape velocity on earth. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocityve for the Earth is given by

ve= 2GMeRe.......................(1)

where, G is the Universal Gravitational constant, Me is the mass and Re is the radius of Earth.

The formula for the escape velocity vesc of any planet is given by

vesc= 2GMR........................(2)

where, M is the mass and R is the radius of the planet.

Given that the mass of the planet is 9 times the mass of the Earth and the radius of the planet is 2 times the radius of the Earth.

Substitute Me9 for M and Re2 for R into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the escape velocity for the planet in terms of that in Earth.

vesc= 2GMe9Re2= 292GMeRe= 23ve.....................(3)

Comparing equation (3) with the given expression, it can be written that

x= 2.

subject: Physics
q: For a body projected at an angle with the horizontal from the ground, choose the correct statement
question: For a body projected at an angle with the horizontal from the ground, choose the correct statement
options: Gravitational potential energy is maximum at the highest point.
The horizontal component of velocity is zero at highest point.
The vertical component of momentum is maximum at the highest point.
The kinetic energy (K.E.) is zero at the highest point of projectile motion.
solution:
In a projectile motion, a particle has two components of velocity. The horizontal component of velocity remains constant over time, while the vertical component of velocity changes.

At highest point, the vertical component of velocity vy becomes zero, but the horizontal component is non-zero. So the kinetic energy of the particle is not zero.

The formula to calculate the potential energy U of the particle is given by

U= mgh...................(1)

where, m is the mass, g is the acceleration due to gravity and h is the vertical height attained by the object.

Hence, the potential energy is maximum when the projectile attains the maximum height.

subject: Physics
q:
The length of a seconds pendulum at a height h=2R from earth surface will be:

(Given: R= Radius of earth and acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth g=π2 m s-2)
question:
The length of a seconds pendulum at a height h=2R from earth surface will be:

(Given: R= Radius of earth and acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth g=π2 m s-2)
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at height 2R from the surface of the Earth will be,

g'=gR2R+h2⇒g'=gR2R+2R2⇒g'=g9

Length of the second's pendulum at the surface of the Earth is 1 m.

Since, time period of a pendulum is given by,

T=2πlg⇒T'T=l'l×gg'⇒1=l'l×gg'⇒l'=l9=19 m

q: A solid sphere of radius R gravitationally attracts a particle placed at 3R from its centre with a force F1. Now a spherical cavity of radius R2 is made in the sphere (as shown in figure) and the force becomes F2. The value of F1:F2 is:
question: A solid sphere of radius R gravitationally attracts a particle placed at 3R from its centre with a force F1. Now a spherical cavity of radius R2 is made in the sphere (as shown in figure) and the force becomes F2. The value of F1:F2 is:
solution:
Let the initial mass of the sphere is m'. Hence, mass of
a removed portion will be m'/8,F1=m.E.=m.Gm'9R2

F2=mG·m'3R2-G·m'/85R/22=Gm'9R2-Gm'×48×25=19-150Gm'R2

F2=4150×9·Gm'R2⇒F1F2=19×50×941=5041

subject: Physics
q: A heavy ball of mass <math><mi>M</mi></math> is suspended from the ceiling of a car by a light string of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> When the car is at rest, the speed of transverse waves in the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When the car has acceleration <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the wave-speed increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60.5</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> in terms of gravitational acceleration <math><mi>g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is closed to
question: A heavy ball of mass <math><mi>M</mi></math> is suspended from the ceiling of a car by a light string of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> When the car is at rest, the speed of transverse waves in the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When the car has acceleration <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the wave-speed increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60.5</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> in terms of gravitational acceleration <math><mi>g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is closed to
solution:
For standing car, v=Tμ=Mgμ



For accelerating car,

υ′=(Mg)2+(Ma)2μ

⇒  60.560=(Mg)2+(Ma)2Mg

⇒  1+11202=(Mg)2+(Ma)2Mg

⇒  1+2120=1+a2g2

≈2120=12a2g2

∴  a=g4120=g30≈g5

subject: Physics
q: Two identical particles each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> go round a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The angular speed of each particle will be :
question: Two identical particles each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> go round a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The angular speed of each particle will be :
solution:
The gravitational force FG between the particles is given by

FG=Gm22a2= Gm24a2   ...1

The centripetal force FC of each particle can be written as

FC=mω2a   ...2

Under balanced condition, equate equation (1) and equation (2) and simplify to obtain the angular speed of each particle.

mω2a=Gm24a2⇒ω2=Gm4a3⇒ω=Gm4a3

subject: Physics
q: The mass of a spaceship is $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$. It is to be launched from the earth's surface out into free space. The value of ' $\mathrm{g}$ ' and ' $R$ ' (radius of earth) are $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $6400 \mathrm{~km}$ respectively. The required energy for this work will be ;
question: The mass of a spaceship is $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$. It is to be launched from the earth's surface out into free space. The value of ' $\mathrm{g}$ ' and ' $R$ ' (radius of earth) are $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $6400 \mathrm{~km}$ respectively. The required energy for this work will be ;
solution:
To launch the spaceship out into free space, from energy conservation,
$\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}+\mathrm{E}=0$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^2}\right) \mathrm{mR}=\mathrm{mgR}$
$=6.4 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Acceleration due to earth's gravity decreases as you go 'up' or 'down' from earth's surface.

Statement II: Acceleration due to earth's gravity is same at a height 'h' and depth 'd' from earth's surface, if h = d.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer form the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Acceleration due to earth's gravity decreases as you go 'up' or 'down' from earth's surface.

Statement II: Acceleration due to earth's gravity is same at a height 'h' and depth 'd' from earth's surface, if h = d.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer form the options given below
options: Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and II are correct
solution:
At any height, g'=g1-2hR[Given: h≪R]

and at any depth,

g'=g1-dR

Clearly, g' decreases in both cases. But if h=d, then value of g' will not be the same.

Therefore, statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect.

q: Energy required to move a body of mass m from an orbit of radius 2R to 3R is
question: Energy required to move a body of mass m from an orbit of radius 2R to 3R is
solution: $\mathrm{E}=(\mathrm{PE})_{\text {final }}-(\mathrm{PE})_{\text {initial }}=\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{3 \mathrm{R}}+\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{6 \mathrm{R}}$

subject: Physics
q:
If suddenly the gravitational force of attraction between Earth and a satellite revolving around it
becomes zero, then the satellite will
question:
If suddenly the gravitational force of attraction between Earth and a satellite revolving around it
becomes zero, then the satellite will
options: continue to move in its orbit with same velocity
move tangentially to the originally orbit in the same velocity
solution: When gravitational force becomes zero so centripetal force on satellite becomes zero so satellite will escape its round orbit and becomes stationary.

subject: Physics
q: The height in terms of radius of the earth $(R)$, at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\dfrac{g}{9}$, where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity on earth's surface, is ______.
question: The height in terms of radius of the earth $(R)$, at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\dfrac{g}{9}$, where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity on earth's surface, is ______.
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at a height $h$ above the surface of the earth is given by

$g' = \dfrac{g}{\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2}$

Given that $g' = \dfrac{g}{9}$, we get

$\dfrac{g}{9} = \dfrac{g}{\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2}$

$\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2 = 9$

$1 + \dfrac{h}{R} = 3$

$\dfrac{h}{R} = 2$

$h = 2R$

Answer: $2R$
